payload.query: A rich literature in comparative politics argues that political parties shape socio-economic outcomes. Different parties are thus expected to be associated with different social policies, taxation systems, employment patterns, inflationary pressures, and levels of economic inequality. Explain the logical foundations of this literature. Why would different parties prefer different policies? In a second step, develop an argument about the persisting ability of political parties to shape socio-economic outcomes in the modern age. Are parties of different ideological orientations still able to meaningfully differentiate themselves from one another in the socio-economic realm? Why / why not?

articles.title: Who Said It Better? How Trump’s Economic Pitch Echoes Biden’s.
articles.abstract: With the midterms approaching and polls showing unease about the economy, some of President Trump’s arguments about its strength sounded eerily like those of his predecessor.
articles.snippet: With the midterms approaching and polls showing unease about the economy, some of President Trump’s arguments about its strength sounded eerily like those of his predecessor.
articles.byline: By Shane Goldmacher and Reid J. Epstein

articles.title: As the Midterms Begin, Texas Offers a Test Case for Both Parties
articles.abstract: Should Democrats concentrate on swing voters or their base? Can more traditional Republicans win in the MAGA era? Tuesday’s Senate primary in Texas will show the direction the parties are taking.
articles.snippet: Should Democrats concentrate on swing voters or their base? Can more traditional Republicans win in the MAGA era? Tuesday’s Senate primary in Texas will show the direction the parties are taking.

articles.title: Welcome to Wyoming, the Frontier of America’s New Gilded Age
articles.abstract: Jackson, Wyo., has long been a refuge for the rich. But the last five years saw a boom in wealth of a kind never before seen. Across the country, the 2017 tax cuts minted hundreds of new billionaires.
articles.snippet: Jackson, Wyo., has long been a refuge for the rich. But the last five years saw a boom in wealth of a kind never before seen. Across the country, the 2017 tax cuts minted hundreds of new billionaires.
articles.byline: By Katie Benner, Steven Rich, Mike Baker, John Branch and Will Warasila

articles.title: How Trump Decided to Go to War
articles.abstract: President Trump’s embrace of military action in Iran was spurred by an Israeli leader determined to end diplomatic negotiations. Few of the president’s advisers voiced opposition.

articles.title: Inside the Underground Safe Houses Sheltering Immigrants From ICE
articles.abstract: In Springfield, Ohio, some Americans have converted their basements and spare bedrooms into shelters for immigrant families who could be targeted in raids.

articles.abstract: Israel’s air forces shifted their focus back to Iran on Friday after a night of heavy bombardment in Lebanon, which was quickly becoming one of the largest fronts in the regional conflict.

articles.title: Behind Oklahoma Cannabis Farms, New Yorkers With Ties to Beijing
articles.abstract: In China, trafficking marijuana is punishable by death. But in the United States, it has become a lucrative sideline for some favored sons of the motherland.

articles.title: Trump Links His Push for Greenland to Not Winning Nobel Peace Prize
articles.abstract: In a text, President Trump told Norway’s prime minister that he no longer felt obliged to “think purely of Peace” and that the U.S. needed the island for global security.
